scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: a pathway for oxygenated blood to the general body system
Exclude family members from care decisions
Use the same approach as with adult patients
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
scenarios.rationale: a pathway for oxygenated blood to the general body system. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 13)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Exclude family members from care decisions
be followed closely and offered nutritional guidance
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
Use the same approach as with adult patients
scenarios.rationale: be followed closely and offered nutritional guidance. Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 28)

scenarios.question: When caring for pediatric patients, the nurse should:
options: Exclude family members from care decisions
Focus only on the child's physical symptoms
not have a fat-restricted diet because calories and fat are necessary
Use the same approach as with adult patients
scenarios.rationale: not have a fat-restricted diet because calories and fat are necessary . Family-centered, age-appropriate care is essential in pediatrics. (Slide 28)

scenarios.question: What is the most common congenital heart defect?
scenarios.rationale: VSD is most common (25-30% of CHDs). Many close spontaneously. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with a cyanotic heart defect presents with:
scenarios.rationale: Cyanotic defects (Tetralogy, Transposition): right-to-left shunt → deoxygenated blood to systemic circulation. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is 'tet spells' in Tetralogy of Fallot?
options: Sudden cyanotic episodes with crying or feeding
scenarios.rationale: Tet spells: acute cyanosis from increased R→L shunting during stress. Knee-chest position, oxygen, morphine. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: The nurse should place a child in knee-chest position for:
options: Tet spells in Tetralogy of Fallot
scenarios.rationale: Knee-chest position increases systemic vascular resistance, decreasing R→L shunt during cyanotic spells. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the priority assessment in a child with suspected Kawasaki disease?
scenarios.rationale: Kawasaki: fever ≥5 days + 4/5 criteria (rash, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, lymphadenopathy, extremity changes). Risk of coronary aneurysms. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Rheumatic fever is caused by:
scenarios.rationale: Rheumatic fever: autoimmune response to untreated strep throat. Affects heart, joints, brain, skin. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the major complication of untreated strep throat?
scenarios.rationale: Untreated GAS → rheumatic fever (heart damage) or post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis. Treat with penicillin. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A child with heart failure presents with:
scenarios.rationale: HF in children: tachypnea, tachycardia, diaphoresis with feeding, hepatomegaly, weight gain (fluid). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: What is the purpose of prostaglandin E1 in a newborn with duct-dependent lesion?
options: Keep ductus arteriosus open until surgery
scenarios.rationale: PGE1 maintains PDA patency in duct-dependent lesions (HLHS, critical PS) until surgical intervention. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Digoxin is used in pediatric heart failure to:
options: Increase cardiac contractility and slow heart rate
scenarios.rationale: Digoxin: positive inotrope, negative chronotrope. Monitor for toxicity (nausea, bradycardia, vision changes). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: A VSD causes blood to shunt:
options: Left to right, increased pulmonary flow, no cyanosis
No shunting due to high LV pressure
Right to left, decreased pulmonary flow, cyanosis
scenarios.rationale: VSD: L→R shunt (higher LV pressure) → increased pulmonary flow, no cyanosis. Acyanotic defect. (Quizlet Cardio)

scenarios.question: Classic VSD finding on auscultation:
options: BP higher in arms than legs
Loud, harsh murmur with systolic thrill at left sternal border
scenarios.rationale: VSD: loud harsh systolic murmur + thrill at left sternal border. (Quizlet Cardio)

scenarios.question: Coarctation of the aorta causes BP:
scenarios.rationale: Coarctation: BP lower in legs (distal to narrowing) than arms (proximal). Classic sign. (Quizlet Cardio)

scenarios.question: Which defects cause increased pulmonary blood flow?
scenarios.rationale: PDA, ASD: L→R shunts → increased pulmonary flow. TOF, tricuspid atresia: decreased flow. (Quizlet Cardio)

scenarios.question: Manifestations of Tetralogy of Fallot include:
scenarios.rationale: TOF (decreased pulmonary flow): polycythemia, clubbing, FTT, systolic murmur. (Quizlet Cardio)

scenarios.question: Kawasaki disease affects the:
scenarios.rationale: Kawasaki: vasculitis → coronary aneurysms if untreated. IVIG + aspirin. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Rheumatic heart disease results from:
scenarios.rationale: Rheumatic fever → heart valve damage (especially mitral). Prevent with penicillin for strep. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Signs of heart failure in infants include:
options: Tachypnea, poor feeding, diaphoresis with feeding, hepatomegaly
scenarios.rationale: HF in infants: tachypnea, poor feeding, diaphoresis, hepatomegaly, weight gain. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Digoxin toxicity signs include:
scenarios.rationale: Digoxin toxicity: GI (nausea, vomiting), cardiac (bradycardia), visual (halos). (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Acyanotic heart defects include:
scenarios.rationale: Acyanotic (L→R): VSD, ASD, PDA, coarctation. Cyanotic (R→L): TOF, transposition, tricuspid atresia. (Standard NCLEX)

scenarios.question: Match each developmental theorist with their key concept.
scenarios.rationale: Erikson: psychosocial crises. Piaget: cognitive development. Kohlberg: moral reasoning. (Slide 15-20)

scenarios.question: Complete the statement about pediatric care.
scenarios.cloze_text: Pediatric nursing requires [[BLANK1]] care and consideration of [[BLANK2]] in all interactions.
scenarios.rationale: Family-centered, age-appropriate care is fundamental in pediatrics. (Slide 10)

scenarios.question: Click on the normal developmental findings.
scenarios.highlight_text: The child demonstrates age-appropriate milestones. Growth follows expected percentiles. The child engages with caregivers appropriately.
scenarios.rationale: All are normal pediatric findings. (Slide 25)

scenarios.question: A child presents with concerning developmental findings.
scenarios.rationale: Early identification and family support are key. (Slide 30)
